title: Dark Tourism
description: For questions 9 – 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Dark tourism refers (0) to travel to places associated with death, tragedy, or disaster. While this might seem morbid, it has become increasingly popular, with millions of people visiting sites like former concentration camps, battlefields, and disaster zones each year.||People visit dark tourism sites (9) for various reasons. Some seek to pay respects to victims, while others want to experience historical events (10) rather than simply reading books or watching films about them. Educational purposes are often cited, (11) as these sites offer powerful lessons about human suffering and resilience.||Famous dark tourism destinations include the Auschwitz concentration camp in Poland, the 9/11 Memorial in New York, and Chernobyl (12) in Ukraine. Guides at these sites must strike a delicate balance between providing information and maintaining appropriate respect. There is (13) always a risk of the experience becoming too commercialised or sensationalised, which critics argue would be disrespectful to those who suffered.||Ethical questions surround dark tourism. Some believe that visiting these places helps ensure (14) that tragic events are not forgotten and similar atrocities won't be repeated. Others worry that taking selfies at sites of tragedy shows (15) a lack of respect. Tourism authorities are increasingly providing guidelines about appropriate behaviour at sensitive sites. (16) Whether dark tourism is educational or exploitative may depend largely on the attitudes and motivations of individual visitors.

example.text: Dark tourism refers (0) travel to places associated with death and tragedy.

questions.explanation: Preposition 'for' introduces the reasons for the visits.
Part of the fixed expression 'rather than', used to introduce a contrast or preference.
Preposition 'for' introduces the reasons for the visits.
Indefinite article: 'shows a lack of respect'.
Conjunction 'that' introduces a noun clause after 'ensure'.
Indefinite article: 'shows a lack of respect'.
Conjunction 'Whether' introduces an indirect question about nature.
